content:
# Exodus Chapter 26 -- The SOPHIA Bible (Consensus)
**Arbiter:** SOPHIA | **In council with:** EMET, LOGOS, PNEUMA
**Source Manuscript:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), Folios ~47r-48r (PDF pp. 93-95); Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209), pp. 68-69 (LXX) | **Source Image:** `source-texts/hebrew-OT/EXO_026_page_001.jpg`, `EXO_026_page_002.jpg`, `EXO_026_page_003.jpg`; `source-texts/greek-OT-LXX/EXO_026_vaticanus_page_001.jpg`, `EXO_026_vaticanus_page_002.jpg`

---

> **MANUSCRIPT NOTE -- THE MISHKAN INSTRUCTIONS:** The Leningrad Codex presents Exodus 26 in standard prose column format with full Masoretic vocalization and cantillation. This chapter forms the architectural core of the tabernacle instructions, moving concentrically from the innermost textile layer outward: the linen curtains of the mishkan proper (vv. 1-6), the goat-hair tent covering (vv. 7-14), the acacia-wood frame structure (vv. 15-30), the inner veil separating the Holy Place from the Holy of Holies (vv. 31-35), and the entrance screen (vv. 36-37). The Hebrew term *mishkan* ("dwelling-place," from *shakan*, "to dwell, to tent") dominates the chapter -- it is the structure that houses the divine Presence. The chapter's concentric organization mirrors its theology: holiness radiates outward from the ark, and the architecture creates graduated zones of access, encoded in a consistent material hierarchy (gold inward, bronze outward; *choshev*-work inward, *roqem*-work outward). The Vaticanus manuscript (pp. 68-69) presents the LXX in continuous uncial script; the versification aligns with the MT throughout, though the LXX characteristically renders *shittim* ("acacia") as *asepta* ("incorruptible") and *qeresh* ("frame") as *stulos* ("pillar").

---

## The Inner Curtains of the Tabernacle (vv. 1-6)

**26:1** "And the tabernacle you shall make with ten curtains of fine twisted linen and blue and purple and scarlet yarn; cherubim, the work of a skilled craftsman, you shall make them. [fn1]

**26:2** The length of each curtain shall be twenty-eight cubits, and the width of each curtain four cubits; one measure for all the curtains. [fn2]

**26:3** Five of the curtains shall be joined each to her sister, and five curtains joined each to her sister. [fn3]

**26:4** And you shall make loops of blue on the edge of the one curtain at the end of the first coupling, and likewise you shall make on the edge of the outermost curtain in the second coupling. [fn4]

**26:5** Fifty loops you shall make on the one curtain, and fifty loops you shall make on the end of the curtain that is in the second coupling; the loops corresponding each to her sister. [fn5]

**26:6** And you shall make fifty clasps of gold, and you shall join the curtains each to her sister with the clasps, and the tabernacle shall be one. [fn6]

---

## The Goat-Hair Tent Covering (vv. 7-14)

**26:7** "And you shall make curtains of goat hair for a tent over the tabernacle; eleven curtains you shall make them. [fn7]

**26:8** The length of each curtain shall be thirty cubits, and the width of each curtain four cubits; one measure for the eleven curtains. [fn8]

**26:9** And you shall join five of the curtains by themselves and six of the curtains by themselves, and you shall fold over the sixth curtain toward the front of the tent. [fn9]

**26:10** And you shall make fifty loops on the edge of the outermost curtain in the first coupling, and fifty loops on the edge of the curtain of the second coupling. [fn10]

**26:11** And you shall make fifty clasps of bronze, and you shall bring the clasps into the loops, and you shall join the tent, and it shall be one. [fn11]

**26:12** And the overhanging excess of the curtains of the tent -- the half curtain that remains over -- shall hang over the back of the tabernacle. [fn12]

**26:13** And the cubit on this side and the cubit on that side of the excess in the length of the curtains of the tent shall hang over the sides of the tabernacle, on this side and on that side, to cover it. [fn13]

**26:14** And you shall make a covering for the tent of ram skins dyed red, and a covering of takhash skins above. [fn14]

---

## The Acacia Frames of the Tabernacle (vv. 15-30)

**26:15** "And you shall make the frames for the tabernacle of acacia wood, standing upright. [fn15]

**26:16** Ten cubits the length of each frame, and a cubit and a half the width of each frame. [fn16]

**26:17** Two tenons for each frame, joined each to her sister; so you shall make for all the frames of the tabernacle. [fn17]

**26:18** And you shall make the frames for the tabernacle: twenty frames for the south side, southward. [fn18]

**26:19** And forty sockets of silver you shall make under the twenty frames: two sockets under the one frame for its two tenons, and two sockets under the next frame for its two tenons. [fn19]

**26:20** And for the second side of the tabernacle, on the north side, twenty frames, [fn20]

**26:21** and their forty sockets of silver: two sockets under the one frame and two sockets under the next frame. [fn21]

**26:22** And for the rear of the tabernacle, westward, you shall make six frames. [fn22]

**26:23** And two frames you shall make for the corners of the tabernacle at the rear. [fn23]

**26:24** And they shall be paired from below, and together they shall be complete at its top, unto the one ring; so it shall be for both of them; for the two corners they shall be. [fn24]

**26:25** And there shall be eight frames and their sockets of silver, sixteen sockets: two sockets under the one frame and two sockets under the next frame. [fn25]

**26:26** And you shall make bars of acacia wood: five for the frames of the one side of the tabernacle, [fn26]

**26:27** and five bars for the frames of the second side of the tabernacle, and five bars for the frames of the side of the tabernacle at the rear, westward. [fn27]

**26:28** And the middle bar in the midst of the frames shall run through from end to end. [fn28]

**26:29** And the frames you shall overlay with gold, and their rings you shall make of gold, as holders for the bars; and you shall overlay the bars with gold. [fn29]

**26:30** And you shall erect the tabernacle according to its plan which you were shown on the mountain. [fn30]

---

## The Veil -- The Parokhet (vv. 31-35)

**26:31** "And you shall make a veil of blue and purple and scarlet yarn and fine twisted linen; the work of a skilled craftsman he shall make it, with cherubim. [fn31]

**26:32** And you shall hang it upon four pillars of acacia overlaid with gold; their hooks of gold, upon four sockets of silver. [fn32]

**26:33** And you shall hang the veil under the clasps, and you shall bring there, within the veil, the ark of the testimony; and the veil shall separate for you between the Holy Place and the Holy of Holies. [fn33]

**26:34** And you shall put the mercy seat upon the ark of the testimony in the Holy of Holies. [fn34]

**26:35** And you shall set the table outside the veil, and the lampstand opposite the table on the south side of the tabernacle; and the table you shall put on the north side. [fn35]

---

## The Screen for the Entrance (vv. 36-37)

**26:36** "And you shall make a screen for the entrance of the tent, of blue and purple and scarlet yarn and fine twisted linen, the work of an embroiderer. [fn36]

**26:37** And you shall make for the screen five pillars of acacia, and you shall overlay them with gold, and their hooks of gold; and you shall cast for them five sockets of bronze. [fn37]

---

## Translator's Notes

### Structural Note

Exodus 26 forms the architectural heart of the tabernacle instruction section (chs. 25-31). While chapter 25 described the sacred furnishings (ark, table, lampstand), chapter 26 describes the structure that houses them -- the dwelling itself. The chapter moves concentrically from inside to outside and from top to bottom: (1) the inner linen curtains with their woven cherubim (vv. 1-6), (2) the outer goat-hair curtains (vv. 7-13), (3) the protective skin coverings (v. 14), (4) the acacia-wood frames (vv. 15-30), (5) the veil separating the two chambers (vv. 31-35), and (6) the entrance screen (vv. 36-37). This ordering is architecturally reversed from what a builder would follow -- one would erect the frames first, then drape the curtains -- but it follows the same divine-perspective logic as chapter 25: God describes the tabernacle beginning with what is nearest to his Presence (the cherubim-adorned inner curtains visible from the divine throne) and moving outward.

The chapter's internal symmetry is notable. The inner curtains are joined by fifty gold clasps (v. 6); the outer curtains by fifty bronze clasps (v. 11). The inner curtains number ten (arranged as five plus five); the outer curtains number eleven (arranged as five plus six). The frame section proceeds by compass direction: south (vv. 18-19), north (vv. 20-21), west/rear (vv. 22-25), unified by the crossbar system (vv. 26-28). The veil section (vv. 31-35) establishes the crucial internal division, while the screen section (vv. 36-37) establishes the boundary between inside and outside. A consistent material hierarchy encodes graduated holiness: gold clasps join the inner curtains, bronze the outer; silver sockets support the veil's pillars, bronze sockets the screen's pillars; *choshev*-work (skilled weaving) adorns the innermost curtains and the veil, *roqem*-work (embroidery) adorns the entrance screen.

### Key Decisions

**"Tabernacle" for *mishkan* (throughout):** EMET renders "tabernacle," LOGOS renders "Tabernacle" (capitalized), PNEUMA renders "dwelling place." The consensus follows EMET/LOGOS -- "tabernacle" for *mishkan* preserves the established English term for this specific structure, which carries its own biblical-theological weight. PNEUMA's "dwelling place," while etymologically closer to the Hebrew root *shakan* ("to dwell"), is a two-word phrase that reads more as a gloss than a name. The term *mishkan* is distinguished from *ohel* ("tent") throughout the chapter: the *mishkan* is the inner fabric structure, the *ohel* is the goat-hair covering over it. Both English terms ("tabernacle" and "tent") must be preserved to maintain this distinction.

**"Skilled craftsman" for *choshev* (vv. 1, 31):** All three translators agree on "skilled craftsman" or equivalent. The Hebrew *choshev* (qal participle of *chashav*, "to think, devise, plan") denotes the highest grade of textile artisan -- literally a "thinker/designer" who weaves figures into the fabric itself, as opposed to the *roqem* ("embroiderer," v. 36) who applies designs to the surface. The distinction *ma'aseh choshev* vs. *ma'aseh roqem* marks a hierarchy of craftsmanship corresponding to the hierarchy of sacred space: the innermost curtains and the veil are *choshev*-work with cherubim; the entrance screen is *roqem*-work without cherubim.

**"Frames" for *qerashim* (vv. 15-30):** EMET renders "frames," LOGOS renders "frames," PNEUMA renders "frames." All three translators agree on "frames" rather than the traditional "boards" or "planks," reflecting the scholarly consensus that the *qerashim* are open frame structures rather than solid planks (which would be impractically heavy and require more acacia wood than the desert environment could supply). The LXX renders *qeresh* as *stulos* ("pillar, post"), suggesting the ancient translators also understood something other than solid planking.

**"Sockets" for *adanim* (vv. 19-25, 32, 37):** EMET renders "sockets," LOGOS renders "bases," PNEUMA renders "bases." The consensus follows EMET -- "sockets" for *adanim* (from *eden*, "base, pedestal") best captures the functional role of these silver pedestals as receiving housings into which the frame tenons are inserted. "Bases" (LOGOS/PNEUMA) is acceptable but loses the mechanical imagery of a socket that receives a peg. Each socket weighs one talent of silver (38:27), approximately 75 pounds (34 kg).

**"Veil" for *parokhet* (vv. 31-35):** All three translators agree on "veil." The *parokhet* is the inner curtain that separates the Holy Place from the Holy of Holies -- the most theologically significant textile in the tabernacle. The word likely derives from a root meaning "to separate, to screen." Like the innermost curtains (v. 1), the veil bears cherubim woven by the *choshev* technique. The LXX renders *parokhet* as *katapetasma* ("curtain, veil"), the same term used in the Synoptic Gospels for the temple veil torn at the crucifixion (Matt 27:51; Mark 15:38; Luke 23:45).

**"Holy of Holies" for *qodesh ha-qodashim* (vv. 33-34):** EMET renders "Holy of Holies," LOGOS renders "Most Holy Place," PNEUMA renders "most holy place." The consensus follows EMET -- "Holy of Holies" preserves the Hebrew superlative construct (*qodesh ha-qodashim*, "holiness of holinesses"), which mirrors other Hebrew superlatives (*shir ha-shirim*, "song of songs"; *melekh ha-melakhim*, "king of kings"). LOGOS/PNEUMA's "Most Holy Place" is a functional equivalent that loses the grammatical pattern.

**"Takhash skins" for *orot tekhashim* (v. 14):** Consistent with the decision at 25:5 -- the identity of *takhash* is genuinely uncertain, and the term is best left transliterated. PNEUMA's "fine leather" imports an interpretation not in the text.

**"Screen" for *masakh* (vv. 36-37):** All three translators agree on "screen." The *masakh* (from *sakhakh*, "to cover, screen") is the entrance curtain, distinguished from the *parokhet* (inner veil) by both its craft (*roqem*-work, not *choshev*-work) and its support (five pillars in bronze sockets, not four pillars in silver sockets).

**"Each to her sister" for *ishshah el-akhotah* (vv. 3, 5, 6, 17):** EMET preserves the literal idiom. LOGOS renders "each to the other." PNEUMA renders "to one another." The consensus follows EMET -- the feminine reciprocal phrase *ishshah el-akhotah* ("a woman to her sister") is the standard Hebrew idiom for paired joining of feminine-gender nouns (*yeri'ah*, "curtain," is feminine), and preserving it maintains the literary texture and the echo of the masculine counterpart *ish el-akhiv* ("each to his brother") in 25:20. This is not merely an idiom but a deliberate literary choice that constructs the tabernacle as a web of relationships.

### Vaticanus (LXX) Divergences

The Vaticanus text of Exodus 26 (pp. 68-69) preserves the LXX version with several notable divergences from the MT: (1) Throughout, the LXX renders *qeresh* ("frame") as *stulos* ("pillar"), a significant architectural reinterpretation -- pillars and frames produce very different structures. (2) The LXX translates *shittim* ("acacia") consistently as *asepta* ("incorruptible wood"), describing the quality rather than the species. (3) In v. 1, the LXX renders *choshev* ("skilled craftsman/designer") as *huphantos* ("weaver"), losing the etymological connection to *chashav* ("to think, devise") that marks this as the highest textile art. (4) In v. 7, the LXX renders the goat-hair curtains with *derris* ("skin covering, leather curtain"), a term that carries different material connotations than the Hebrew *yeri'ot*. (5) In v. 14, *tekhashim* is rendered *huakinthina* ("hyacinth-colored"), interpreting the unknown animal as a color designation, consistent with the LXX pattern at 25:5. (6) In v. 30, the LXX renders *ke-mishpato* ("according to its plan") as *kata to eidos* ("according to the form/appearance"), reinforcing the pattern-language of 25:9, 40. (7) In v. 31, the LXX renders *parokhet* as *katapetasma* ("curtain that hangs down"), the same term the Synoptic Gospels use for the temple veil torn at the crucifixion -- making the LXX translation of this chapter the direct terminological bridge between the Exodus tabernacle instructions and the passion narratives. (8) In v. 33, the LXX uses *diorieo* ("divide, define") for *hivdilah*, maintaining the separation concept but losing the specific verbal echo of Genesis 1. (9) In v. 36, the LXX preserves the *choshev*/*roqem* distinction: the entrance screen is *poikiltou* ("of variegated/embroidered work") rather than *huphantos* ("of woven work").

---

> **[fn1] v.1 -- *Ve-et-ha-mishkan ta'aseh eser yeri'ot shesh moshzar u-tekhelet ve-argaman ve-tola'at shani keruvim ma'aseh choshev ta'aseh otam*: "And the tabernacle you shall make with ten curtains of fine twisted linen and blue and purple and scarlet yarn; cherubim, the work of a skilled craftsman, you shall make them"** -- All three translators agree in substance. EMET renders "the tabernacle you shall make," LOGOS renders "the Tabernacle you shall make," PNEUMA renders "Make the dwelling place itself out of ten curtains." The consensus follows EMET/LOGOS, preserving the Hebrew word order (*ve-et-ha-mishkan ta'aseh*), which foregrounds the tabernacle as grammatical object before the verb. PNEUMA restructures to English natural order and substitutes "dwelling place" for "tabernacle" (see Key Decisions). For *ma'aseh choshev*, all render "the work of a skilled craftsman" or equivalent. The *choshev* technique produces tapestry weaving where the cherubim design is integral to the fabric itself, not applied afterward. The cherubim woven into these innermost curtains would be the only decoration visible from inside the sanctuary -- the priests looking up would see winged figures in the ceiling fabric.

> **[fn2] v.2 -- *Orekh ha-yeri'ah ha-akhat shemoneh ve-esrim ba-ammah ve-rokhav arba ba-ammah ha-yeri'ah ha-akhat middah akhat le-khol ha-yeri'ot*: "The length of each curtain shall be twenty-eight cubits, and the width of each curtain four cubits; one measure for all the curtains"** -- All three translators agree. Each curtain measures 28 cubits long by 4 cubits wide (approximately 42 x 6 feet). Ten curtains at 4 cubits each produce a total width of 40 cubits. *Middah akhat* ("one measure") -- all curtains are identical in size.

> **[fn3] v.3 -- *Chamesh ha-yeri'ot tihyeynah chovrot ishshah el-akhotah ve-chamesh yeri'ot chovrot ishshah el-akhotah*: "Five of the curtains shall be joined each to her sister, and five curtains joined each to her sister"** -- EMET preserves "each to her sister." LOGOS renders "each to the other." PNEUMA renders "to one another." The consensus follows EMET (see Key Decisions, "each to her sister"). The ten curtains form two sets of five, which are then joined by gold clasps (v. 6) to create the unified covering. The participle *chovrot* (qal feminine plural of *chavar*, "to join, bind") indicates the panels are sewn or connected edge to edge within each set.

> **[fn4] v.4 -- *Ve-asita lul'ot tekhelet al sefat ha-yeri'ah ha-akhat mi-qatseh ba-chovaret ve-khen ta'aseh bi-sfat ha-yeri'ah ha-qitsonah ba-makhberet ha-shenit*: "And you shall make loops of blue on the edge of the one curtain at the end of the first coupling, and likewise you shall make on the edge of the outermost curtain in the second coupling"** -- All three translators agree in substance. EMET renders "first coupling...second coupling." LOGOS renders "first coupling...second coupling." PNEUMA renders "first set...second set." The consensus follows EMET/LOGOS -- "coupling" for *chovaret*/*makhberet* (both from *chavar*, "to join") preserves the root meaning. PNEUMA's "set" loses the verbal force of the Hebrew terms, which denote an assembled, joined group.

> **[fn5] v.5 -- *Chamishim lul'ot ta'aseh ba-yeri'ah ha-akhat va-chamishim lul'ot ta'aseh bi-qtseh ha-yeri'ah asher ba-makhberet ha-shenit maqbilot ha-lul'ot ishshah el-akhotah*: "Fifty loops you shall make on the one curtain, and fifty loops you shall make on the end of the curtain that is in the second coupling; the loops corresponding each to her sister"** -- EMET renders "the loops receiving each to her sister." LOGOS renders "the loops shall be opposite one to another." PNEUMA renders "the loops directly opposite each other." The consensus renders "corresponding" for *maqbilot* (hiphil participle of *qaval*, "to receive, correspond") -- "corresponding" captures both the spatial alignment and the functional matching better than EMET's "receiving" (too mechanical) or LOGOS/PNEUMA's "opposite" (which implies only position, not the functional correspondence the Hebrew connotes).

> **[fn6] v.6 -- *Ve-asita chamishim qarsei zahav ve-khibbarta et-ha-yeri'ot ishshah el-akhotah ba-qerasim ve-hayah ha-mishkan ekhad*: "And you shall make fifty clasps of gold, and you shall join the curtains each to her sister with the clasps, and the tabernacle shall be one"** -- All three translators agree. The gold clasps joining the innermost curtains contrast with the bronze clasps of v. 11 for the outer tent -- materials correspond to proximity to the divine Presence. The theological point *ve-hayah ha-mishkan ekhad* ("and the tabernacle shall be one") -- the two halves, once clasped, become a unified whole. The gold clasps number fifty, the number of jubilee.

> **[fn7] v.7 -- *Ve-asita yeri'ot izzim le-ohel al-ha-mishkan ashtei-esreh yeri'ot ta'aseh otam*: "And you shall make curtains of goat hair for a tent over the tabernacle; eleven curtains you shall make them"** -- All three translators agree. The *ohel* ("tent") is distinguished from the *mishkan* ("tabernacle/dwelling") beneath it: the *mishkan* is the beautiful inner fabric with woven cherubim, the *ohel* is the functional goat-hair covering that protects it. The eleven curtains (one more than the inner set) provide extra coverage.

> **[fn8] v.8 -- *Orekh ha-yeri'ah ha-akhat sheloshim ba-ammah ve-rokhav arba ba-ammah ha-yeri'ah ha-akhat middah akhat le-ashtei esreh yeri'ot*: "The length of each curtain shall be thirty cubits, and the width of each curtain four cubits; one measure for the eleven curtains"** -- All three translators agree. The goat-hair curtains are 30 cubits long (two cubits longer than the inner linen curtains), providing overhang on each side.

> **[fn9] v.9 -- *Ve-khibbarta et-chamesh ha-yeri'ot le-vad ve-et-shesh ha-yeri'ot le-vad ve-khafalta et-ha-yeri'ah ha-shishit el-mul penei ha-ohel*: "And you shall join five of the curtains by themselves and six of the curtains by themselves, and you shall fold over the sixth curtain toward the front of the tent"** -- EMET renders "fold over." LOGOS renders "double over." PNEUMA renders "fold...double." The consensus follows EMET -- "fold over" for *ve-khafalta* (qal perfect of *kafal*, "to fold, double over") is the most natural English rendering. The goat-hair curtains are divided into unequal sets of five and six (not equal halves like the inner curtains), and the sixth curtain is folded at the entrance, creating a reinforced or decorative drape.

> **[fn10] v.10 -- *Ve-asita chamishim lul'ot al sefat ha-yeri'ah ha-akhat ha-qitsonah ba-choveret va-chamishim lul'ot al sefat ha-yeri'ah ha-shenit ha-makhbaret*: "And you shall make fifty loops on the edge of the outermost curtain in the first coupling, and fifty loops on the edge of the curtain of the second coupling"** -- All three translators agree. Same loop-and-clasp joining system as the inner curtains (vv. 4-5), now for the goat-hair layer.

> **[fn11] v.11 -- *Ve-asita qarsei nekhoshet chamishim ve-heveta et-ha-qerasim ba-lul'ot ve-khibbarta et-ha-ohel ve-hayah ekhad*: "And you shall make fifty clasps of bronze, and you shall bring the clasps into the loops, and you shall join the tent, and it shall be one"** -- All three translators agree. Bronze clasps for the outer tent, not gold (cf. v. 6). The material hierarchy reflects the graduated holiness of the layers. *Ve-hayah ekhad* ("and it shall be one") parallels v. 6 -- the tent, like the tabernacle, achieves unity through joining.

> **[fn12] v.12 -- *Ve-serekh ha-odef bi-yeri'ot ha-ohel chatsi ha-yeri'ah ha-odefet tisrakh al akhorei ha-mishkan*: "And the overhanging excess of the curtains of the tent -- the half curtain that remains over -- shall hang over the back of the tabernacle"** -- All three translators agree in substance. EMET renders "overhanging part, the excess." LOGOS renders "overhanging excess." PNEUMA renders "extra length that hangs over." The consensus follows LOGOS for conciseness. *Serekh* ("overhang," from *sarakh*, "to hang down") and *odef* ("excess," from *adaf*, "to be in excess") together describe the surplus fabric at the rear.

> **[fn13] v.13 -- *Ve-ha-ammah mi-zeh ve-ha-ammah mi-zeh ba-odef be-orekh yeri'ot ha-ohel yihyeh sarukh al-tsiddei ha-mishkan mi-zeh u-mi-zeh le-khassoto*: "And the cubit on this side and the cubit on that side of the excess in the length of the curtains of the tent shall hang over the sides of the tabernacle, on this side and on that side, to cover it"** -- All three translators agree. The extra two cubits of length (30 vs. 28) allow one cubit of overhang on each side, providing additional protection. *Le-khassoto* (piel infinitive construct of *kasah*, "to cover" + 3ms suffix) -- "to cover it."

> **[fn14] v.14 -- *Ve-asita mikhseh la-ohel orot eilim me'oddamim u-mikhseh orot tekhashim mi-lema'lah*: "And you shall make a covering for the tent of ram skins dyed red, and a covering of takhash skins above"** -- EMET renders "skins of rams made red...skins of takhash." LOGOS renders "rams' skins dyed red...tahash skins." PNEUMA renders "ram skins dyed red...fine leather." The consensus renders "ram skins dyed red...takhash skins" -- simplifying word order while preserving *takhash* untranslated (see Key Decisions). PNEUMA's "fine leather" for *orot tekhashim* is rejected as interpretive. The four-layer structure from inside out: (1) linen curtains with cherubim, (2) goat-hair tent curtains, (3) red-dyed ram skins, (4) takhash skins on top.

> **[fn15] v.15 -- *Ve-asita et-ha-qerashim la-mishkan atsei shittim omedim*: "And you shall make the frames for the tabernacle of acacia wood, standing upright"** -- All three translators agree (see Key Decisions, "frames" for *qerashim*). The participle *omedim* (qal of *amad*, "to stand") -- "standing, upright"; the frames stand vertically. The LXX renders *qeresh* as *stulos* ("pillar"), a significant architectural reinterpretation.

> **[fn16] v.16 -- *Eser ammot orekh ha-qaresh ve-ammah va-chetsi ha-ammah rokhav ha-qaresh ha-ekhad*: "Ten cubits the length of each frame, and a cubit and a half the width of each frame"** -- All three translators agree. Each frame is 10 cubits tall (approximately 15 feet) by 1.5 cubits wide (approximately 2.25 feet).

> **[fn17] v.17 -- *Shtei yadot la-qaresh ha-ekhad meshullavot ishshah el-akhotah ken ta'aseh le-khol qarshei ha-mishkan*: "Two tenons for each frame, joined each to her sister; so you shall make for all the frames of the tabernacle"** -- All three translators agree. *Yadot* ("tenons," literally "hands") -- projecting pegs at the base of each frame that fit into the silver sockets. *Meshullavot* (pual participle of *shalav*, "to join, set in order") -- the two tenons are parallel and aligned.

> **[fn18] v.18 -- *Ve-asita et-ha-qerashim la-mishkan esrim qeresh li-fe'at negbah teimanah*: "And you shall make the frames for the tabernacle: twenty frames for the south side, southward"** -- All three translators agree. *Negbah teimanah* -- a double directional indicator: *negev* = "south" (literally "dry land"), *teimanah* = "to the right hand" (south when facing east). Twenty frames at 1.5 cubits each = 30 cubits total south wall length.

> **[fn19] v.19 -- *Ve-arba'im adnei-khesef ta'aseh takhat esrim ha-qerashim shnei adanim takhat ha-qaresh ha-ekhad li-shtei yadotav u-shnei adanim takhat ha-qaresh ha-ekhad li-shtei yadotav*: "And forty sockets of silver you shall make under the twenty frames: two sockets under the one frame for its two tenons, and two sockets under the next frame for its two tenons"** -- EMET renders "sockets." LOGOS renders "bases." PNEUMA renders "silver bases." The consensus follows EMET -- "sockets" (see Key Decisions). The second occurrence of *ha-qaresh ha-ekhad* is rendered "the next frame" rather than repeating "the one frame" to avoid confusion in English while preserving the distributive sense.

> **[fn20] v.20 -- *U-le-tsela ha-mishkan ha-shenit li-fe'at tsafon esrim qaresh*: "And for the second side of the tabernacle, on the north side, twenty frames"** -- All three translators agree. *Tsela* ("side, rib") -- the same word used for Adam's "rib" (Gen 2:21-22); here, the structural side-wall.

> **[fn21] v.21 -- *Ve-arba'im adneihem khesef shnei adanim takhat ha-qaresh ha-ekhad u-shnei adanim takhat ha-qaresh ha-ekhad*: "And their forty sockets of silver: two sockets under the one frame and two sockets under the next frame"** -- All three translators agree. The north wall mirrors the south wall exactly.

> **[fn22] v.22 -- *U-le-yarketei ha-mishkan yammah ta'aseh shishah qerashim*: "And for the rear of the tabernacle, westward, you shall make six frames"** -- All three translators agree. *Yarketei* ("rear parts," from *yarekh*, "thigh, rear") -- the back (western) end of the tabernacle. *Yammah* ("seaward, westward," from *yam*, "sea") -- the Mediterranean lay to the west. Six frames at 1.5 cubits = 9 cubits width.

> **[fn23] v.23 -- *U-shnei qerashim ta'aseh li-mequtse'ot ha-mishkan ba-yarketayim*: "And two frames you shall make for the corners of the tabernacle at the rear"** -- All three translators agree. *Mequtse'ot* ("corners," from *qatsa*, "to cut off") -- specially shaped corner-frames where the rear wall meets the side walls.

> **[fn24] v.24 -- *Ve-yihyu to'amim mi-lemattah ve-yakhdav yihyu tammim al-rosho el-ha-tabba'at ha-akhat ken yihyeh li-shneihem li-shnei ha-miqtso'ot yihyu*: "And they shall be paired from below, and together they shall be complete at its top, unto the one ring; so it shall be for both of them; for the two corners they shall be"** -- EMET renders "paired." LOGOS renders "doubled." PNEUMA renders "joined at the bottom and fitted together at the top." The consensus follows EMET -- "paired" for *to'amim* (from *ta'am*, "to be twin") captures the twinning concept better than LOGOS's "doubled" (which suggests mere duplication) or PNEUMA's paraphrase. *Tammim* ("complete, whole," from *tamam*) -- the paired corner frames form a unified whole at the top, fastened with a single ring.

> **[fn25] v.25 -- *Ve-hayu shemonah qerashim ve-adneihem kesef shishah asar adanim shnei adanim takhat ha-qaresh ha-ekhad u-shnei adanim takhat ha-qaresh ha-ekhad*: "And there shall be eight frames and their sockets of silver, sixteen sockets: two sockets under the one frame and two sockets under the next frame"** -- All three translators agree. The west wall has eight frames total (six regular plus two corner-frames), with sixteen silver sockets. Total sockets: 40 (south) + 40 (north) + 16 (west) = 96 silver sockets for the frame structure.

> **[fn26] v.26 -- *Ve-asita verikhai atsei shittim chamishah le-qarshei tsela ha-mishkan ha-ekhad*: "And you shall make bars of acacia wood: five for the frames of the one side of the tabernacle"** -- EMET renders "bars." LOGOS renders "bars." PNEUMA renders "crossbars." The consensus follows EMET/LOGOS -- "bars" for *berikhai* (from *beriakh*, "bar, bolt"). PNEUMA's "crossbars" is acceptable but adds a directional specificity ("cross-") not in the Hebrew. Five bars per wall provide structural rigidity.

> **[fn27] v.27 -- *Va-chamishah verikkim le-qarshei tsela ha-mishkan ha-shenit va-chamishah verikkim le-qarshei tsela ha-mishkan la-yarketayim yammah*: "And five bars for the frames of the second side of the tabernacle, and five bars for the frames of the side of the tabernacle at the rear, westward"** -- All three translators agree. Fifteen bars total: five for each of the three walls (south, north, west).

> **[fn28] v.28 -- *Ve-ha-beriakh ha-tikhon be-tokh ha-qerashim mavriakh min-ha-qatseh el-ha-qatseh*: "And the middle bar in the midst of the frames shall run through from end to end"** -- EMET renders "shall run through." LOGOS renders "shall run." PNEUMA renders "is to extend." The consensus follows EMET -- "shall run through" for *mavriakh* (hiphil participle of *barakh*, "to flee, pass through") captures the active, penetrating motion of the bar passing through each frame. The middle bar of the five extends the full length of the wall (Talmud, Shabbat 98b: it ran through holes bored in the middle of each frame).

> **[fn29] v.29 -- *Ve-et-ha-qerashim tetsappeh zahav ve-et-tabbeoteihem ta'aseh zahav batim la-berikkim ve-tsippita et-ha-berikkim zahav*: "And the frames you shall overlay with gold, and their rings you shall make of gold, as holders for the bars; and you shall overlay the bars with gold"** -- All three translators agree. *Batim* ("houses, holders," from *bayit*, "house") -- the rings serve as housings through which the bars pass (cf. 25:27). The gold-overlaid frames and bars form the glittering interior walls visible through the linen curtain panels.

> **[fn30] v.30 -- *Va-haqemota et-ha-mishkan ke-mishpato asher horeita ba-har*: "And you shall erect the tabernacle according to its plan which you were shown on the mountain"** -- EMET renders "set up." LOGOS renders "erect." PNEUMA renders "set up." The consensus follows LOGOS -- "erect" for *va-haqemota* (hiphil perfect of *qum*, "to arise, stand") captures the causative force ("cause to stand") more precisely. *Ke-mishpato* ("according to its plan/ordinance," from *mishpat*, "judgment, ordinance, manner") -- the building plan has the force of legal decree. *Horeita* (hophal perfect of *ra'ah*, "to see") -- "you were shown, you were caused to see"; Moses passively received the divine vision. This echoes 25:9, 40.

> **[fn31] v.31 -- *Ve-asita farokhet tekhelet ve-argaman ve-tola'at shani ve-shesh moshzar ma'aseh choshev ya'aseh otah keruvim*: "And you shall make a veil of blue and purple and scarlet yarn and fine twisted linen; the work of a skilled craftsman he shall make it, with cherubim"** -- All three translators agree. The *parokhet* is the inner veil separating the Holy Place from the Holy of Holies. Like the innermost curtains (v. 1), it is woven with cherubim by the *ma'aseh choshev* technique -- the highest craftsmanship. The material list here reverses the order of v. 1 (blue-purple-scarlet first, then linen), perhaps indicating the colored threads are more prominent in the veil than the linen ground.

> **[fn32] v.32 -- *Ve-natattah otah al-arba'ah ammudei shittim metsuppim zahav vaveihem zahav al-arba'ah adnei-khasef*: "And you shall hang it upon four pillars of acacia overlaid with gold; their hooks of gold, upon four sockets of silver"** -- EMET renders "put." LOGOS renders "hang." PNEUMA renders "hang." The consensus follows LOGOS/PNEUMA -- "hang" for *ve-natattah* (from *natan*, "to give, put, set") captures the specific action of suspending the veil from hooks on the pillars. *Vaveihem* ("their hooks," from *vav*, "hook") -- the letter vav in the Hebrew alphabet is named for this hook shape. Silver sockets for the veil pillars (contrast with the bronze sockets of the entrance screen, v. 37 -- the material hierarchy continues).

> **[fn33] v.33 -- *Ve-natattah et-ha-parokhet takhat ha-qerasim ve-heveta shammah mi-beit la-parokhet et aron ha-edut ve-hivdilah ha-parokhet lakhem bein ha-qodesh u-vein qodesh ha-qodashim*: "And you shall hang the veil under the clasps, and you shall bring there, within the veil, the ark of the testimony; and the veil shall separate for you between the Holy Place and the Holy of Holies"** -- All three translators agree in substance. The veil is positioned directly beneath the fifty gold clasps that join the two halves of the linen curtains (v. 6) -- the seam-line above marks the boundary-line below. *Ve-hivdilah* (hiphil perfect of *badal*, "to separate, divide") -- the same verb used in creation (Gen 1:4, 6, 7, 14, 18) to divide light from darkness, waters from waters. The parokhet is a creation-boundary within the sanctuary. For *qodesh ha-qodashim*, see Key Decisions ("Holy of Holies" preferred over "Most Holy Place").

> **[fn34] v.34 -- *Ve-natatta et-ha-kapporet al aron ha-edut be-qodesh ha-qodashim*: "And you shall put the mercy seat upon the ark of the testimony in the Holy of Holies"** -- All three translators agree. The *kapporet* is placed on the ark, and the ensemble resides in the innermost chamber.

> **[fn35] v.35 -- *Ve-samta et-ha-shulkhan mi-khuts la-parokhet ve-et-ha-menorah nokhakh ha-shulkhan al tsela ha-mishkan teimanah ve-ha-shulkhan titten al tsela tsafon*: "And you shall set the table outside the veil, and the lampstand opposite the table on the south side of the tabernacle; and the table you shall put on the north side"** -- All three translators agree. The spatial arrangement: the table of the Bread of the Presence on the north side, the lampstand on the south side, facing each other across the Holy Place. The altar of incense (30:1-6) will later be placed between them, before the veil. *Nokhakh* ("opposite, facing") -- the lampstand and table are in direct visual relationship.

> **[fn36] v.36 -- *Ve-asita masakh le-fetakh ha-ohel tekhelet ve-argaman ve-tola'at shani ve-shesh moshzar ma'aseh roqem*: "And you shall make a screen for the entrance of the tent, of blue and purple and scarlet yarn and fine twisted linen, the work of an embroiderer"** -- All three translators agree. *Ma'aseh roqem* ("work of an embroiderer") -- *roqem* (qal participle of *raqam*, "to variegate, embroider") denotes a lower grade of textile work than *choshev* (vv. 1, 31); embroidery is applied to the surface, while *choshev*-weaving is integral to the fabric. The entrance screen has no cherubim specified -- only the innermost curtains and the parokhet bear the cherub design.

> **[fn37] v.37 -- *Ve-asita la-masakh chamishah ammudei shittim ve-tsippita otam zahav vaveihem zahav ve-yatsaqta lahem chamishah adnei nekhoshet*: "And you shall make for the screen five pillars of acacia, and you shall overlay them with gold, and their hooks of gold; and you shall cast for them five sockets of bronze"** -- All three translators agree. The entrance screen has five pillars (vs. four for the parokhet, v. 32) and bronze sockets (vs. silver for the veil). Gold hooks are used for both, but the foundation material degrades as one moves outward from the Holy of Holies. *Ve-yatsaqta* (qal perfect of *yatsaq*, "to pour, cast") -- the bronze sockets are cast, a detail that specifies the manufacturing process. The material gradient encodes the theology of graduated holiness: the nearer to YHWH's dwelling, the more precious the materials.

---

*Translated from the Leningrad Codex (B19A) with reference to Codex Vaticanus (LXX). All transliterations follow standard academic conventions. Hebrew text follows the Masoretic vocalization.*
